---
title: "Warm-up: the 2×2 inverse"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

- Compute the inverse of a $2\times2$ matrix from one formula.
- Check your answer by confirming $A A^{-1} = I$.
- Solve a small system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ using $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
- Recognise a *singular* matrix ($\det = 0$) that has no inverse.

## The method

For a $2\times2$ matrix the inverse is:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix},
\qquad
A^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}.
$$

Three steps:

1. Compute the determinant $ad - bc$.
2. **Swap** $a$ and $d$; **negate** $b$ and $c$.
3. Divide every entry by the determinant.

If $ad - bc = 0$ you cannot divide — the matrix is **singular** and has **no
inverse**.

The **identity matrix** $I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is the
"1" of matrices: $A A^{-1} = I$ for any invertible $A$.

**Tiny worked example.**
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix},
\quad \det(A) = (2)(1) - (1)(1) = 1.
$$
$$
A^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Check: $A A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I.$ Correct.

## Drills

**Find the inverse** (all have clean determinants).

1. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
2. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
3. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$
4. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
5. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
6. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
7. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
8. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ (determinant $2$)

**Recognise the singular matrix** (no inverse).

9. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ — does an inverse exist?
10. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ — does an inverse exist?

**Verify** $A A^{-1} = I$.

11. For drill 5, multiply $A A^{-1}$ and confirm you get $I$.
12. For drill 6, multiply $A A^{-1}$ and confirm you get $I$.

**Solve** $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ using $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

13. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$.
14. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$.
15. $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$.

## Check in R

The inverse is computed by [`solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html) with a
single argument; the matrix product uses `%*%`. To solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$
directly, pass both: `solve(A, b)`.

```r
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
solve(A)
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    1   -1
# [2,]   -1    2

A %*% solve(A)
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    1    0
# [2,]    0    1      (the identity I)

b <- c(3, 2)
solve(A, b)
# [1] 1 1            (so x1 = 1, x2 = 1)

S <- matrix(c(1, 2,
              2, 4), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
solve(S)
# Error: system is computationally singular   (det = 0, no inverse)
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — the three steps"}
(1) determinant $ad-bc$, (2) swap $a$ and $d$ and negate $b$ and $c$, (3)
divide every entry by the determinant.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — diagonal matrices"}
For $\begin{bmatrix} a & 0 \\ 0 & d \end{bmatrix}$ the inverse is just
$\begin{bmatrix} 1/a & 0 \\ 0 & 1/d \end{bmatrix}$. The off-diagonal zeros stay
zero.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — spotting a singular matrix"}
Compute $ad - bc$ first. If it is $0$, stop: there is no inverse. This happens
when one row (or column) is a multiple of the other.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — verifying the inverse"}
Row $i$ times column $j$: multiply matching entries and add. The diagonal
products should give $1$ and the off-diagonal should give $0$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 5 — solving with the inverse"}
$\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ is a matrix-times-vector product. Top entry of
$\mathbf{x}$ = (first row of $A^{-1}$) $\cdot \mathbf{b}$; bottom entry =
(second row) $\cdot \mathbf{b}$.
:::

## Step-by-step solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solutions"}

**Drill 1.** $\det = (1)(1) - (0)(0) = 1$. Swap/negate, divide by $1$:
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.$ The identity is its own
inverse.

**Drill 2.** $\det = (1)(2) - 0 = 2$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 3.** $\det = (2)(5) - 0 = 10$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{10}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{1}{5} \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 4.** $\det = (1)(1) - (2)(0) = 1$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 5.** $\det = (3)(1) - (1)(2) = 1$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 6.** $\det = (2)(2) - (1)(3) = 1$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 7.** $\det = (4)(1) - (3)(1) = 1$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -3 \\ -1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -3 \\ -1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 8.** $\det = (3)(2) - (2)(2) = 6 - 4 = 2$.
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & \tfrac{3}{2} \end{bmatrix}.$

**Drill 9.** $\det = (1)(4) - (2)(2) = 4 - 4 = 0$. **No inverse** — the matrix
is singular (the second row is $2\times$ the first).

**Drill 10.** $\det = (3)(2) - (6)(1) = 6 - 6 = 0$. **No inverse** — singular
(the first row is $3\times$ the second).

**Drill 11.** Using $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$:
$$
A A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}
(3)(1)+(1)(-2) & (3)(-1)+(1)(3) \\
(2)(1)+(1)(-2) & (2)(-1)+(1)(3)
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I. \checkmark
$$

**Drill 12.** Using $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ and
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$:
$$
A A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}
(2)(2)+(1)(-3) & (2)(-1)+(1)(2) \\
(3)(2)+(2)(-3) & (3)(-1)+(2)(2)
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I. \checkmark
$$

**Drill 13.** $\det = (2)(1)-(1)(1) = 1$, so
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. Then
$$
\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} (1)(3)+(-1)(2) \\ (-1)(3)+(2)(2) \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
So $x_1 = 1,\ x_2 = 1$.

**Drill 14.** $\det = 1$, so
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Then
$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} (1)(5)+(-2)(2) \\ (0)(5)+(1)(2) \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
So $x_1 = 1,\ x_2 = 2$.

**Drill 15.** $\det = (3)(1)-(1)(2) = 1$, so
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. Then
$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} (1)(4)+(-1)(3) \\ (-2)(4)+(3)(3) \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
So $x_1 = 1,\ x_2 = 1$.

:::
